menu.title: EU Puzzle
menu.subtitle: Rebuild the map of the European Union

deviceWarning.title: Device not supported
deviceWarning.description: This puzzle game is currently only available on tablets and desktop computers.

confirm.discardMsg: You have already connected {count} countries. Are you sure you want to restart?
confirm.tutorialMsg: You have already connected {count} countries. Starting the tutorial will reset the game.
confirm.menuMsg: You have connected {count} countries. Going back to the menu will lose your progress.
confirm.accountMsg: You have connected {count} countries. Going to your account page will lose your progress.

welcome.title: European Union Puzzle
welcome.subtitle: Rebuild the EU map by connecting all 27 member countries!

welcome.title: Welcome!
welcome.text: You are here to rebuild the map of the European Union. The 27 countries are scattered and your goal is to put them back in their place! This tutorial will guide you step by step.

board.title: The Game Area
board.text: This is the playing area. The puzzle pieces are scattered around here. Depending on your difficulty, you will need to drag them to their correct spot or snap them together.

snapEasy.title: Drag to the Outline
snapEasy.text: Drag France to its matching outline on the board. The piece will automatically snap into place when you get close enough!

snapMedium.title: Snap the Borders
snapMedium.text: Snap France to Germany. Bring the two countries closer until they join automatically. Golden arrows will show you the direction!

rotateHard.title: Rotate & Snap
rotateHard.text: In Hard mode, pieces are rotated. Right-click or scroll with your mouse over France to rotate it until it's upright, then snap it to Germany!

info.title: Info and Curiosities
info.text: Now that you have placed the piece, click the map pin to discover real information and facts! Note: when you connect countries this tab will open automatically.

progress.title: Progress Bar
progress.text: Here you see how many countries you have already connected. The goal is to bring the counter to 27 out of 27!

settings.title: Settings
settings.text: From the Settings button you can adjust the volume of the music and sound effects, and change the text size for better readability.

reopen.title: Reopen the Tutorial
reopen.text: Have doubts during the game? Press the 'Tutorial' button at the top of the page to reopen this guide at any time!

hint.title: Hint
hint.text: Are you stuck? This button highlights the smallest country not yet connected. Use it sparingly so you don't lose the taste for the challenge!

reset.title: Restart
reset.text: If you want to start over, use the Reset button. All pieces are reshuffled and you can try again at your leisure!

ready.title: You're Ready!
ready.text: Now you know everything you need. Good luck rebuilding the European Union! Remember: you can always reopen this tutorial from the button at the top. You can do it!

error.loadGame: Error loading the game. Please reload the page.

stats.title: Statistics
stats.submittedWithRank: Record saved! You are now #{rank} on the leaderboard.
stats.notImprovedWithRank: No improvement this time. You are currently #{rank}.
stats.noEntries: No entries yet. Be the first!

auth.signInHint: Sign in to save your record to your account.
auth.signInForRecord: Log in to save to account
auth.magicLinkRateLimit: Too many login attempts. Please wait a minute and try again.
auth.magicLinkTemporaryError: Login service is temporarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
auth.magicLinkGenericError: Unable to send the magic link right now. Please try again.
auth.linkSent: Check your inbox — click the magic link to sign in!
auth.setUsernameHint: Set a username to appear on the leaderboard.
auth.setupDesc: Choose a username to appear on the global leaderboard.
auth.banner: Sign in to save your records to your account and appear on the global leaderboard.
auth.loginSubtitle: No password needed — we'll send a magic link to your inbox.
auth.linkSentDesc: Click the magic link in your email to sign in. You can close this tab.
auth.accountRequiresLogin: Sign in to manage your account.
auth.deleteAccountHint: This permanently removes your profile and leaderboard records.
auth.deleteAccountWarn: This action cannot be undone. Are you sure?

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.intro: EU Puzzle stores only what is needed to run the game.
privacy.localItems: Game settings (volume, font size) · Personal best times per difficulty · Language preference · Guest identity (random ID + name)
privacy.cloudTitle: Data sent to the global leaderboard (Supabase)
privacy.cloudItems: Display name (username or Guest_NNNN) · Best completion time · Difficulty · Optional: your country · If signed in: your email address
privacy.authText: Sign-in uses Supabase magic links (email OTP). Your email is stored securely by Supabase and is never shared or sold.
privacy.deleteLocal: Clear your browser's localStorage to remove all local data.
privacy.deleteCloud: Sign in and use the Account page to delete your leaderboard entry.
privacy.contact: Questions? Open an issue on GitHub.

100.name: Bulgaria
facts: In Bulgaria, shaking your head means 'yes' and nodding means 'no'.
The oldest worked gold treasure in the world was found at Varna.
Bulgaria produces 85% of the world's rose oil.
The Cyrillic alphabet originated here.
The yogurt bacterium (Lactobacillus bulgaricus) is only found naturally here.
Plovdiv is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in Europe.
The bagpipe (Gaida) is a national instrument.
Bulgaria is the only European country that has never changed its name since 681 AD.
Computer pioneer John Atanasoff had Bulgarian roots.
Nearly a third of the country is covered by forests.

191.name: Croatia
facts: The modern necktie derives from Croatia (from 17th-century Croatian soldiers).
Game of Thrones' King's Landing was filmed in Dubrovnik.
Nikola Tesla was born in Smiljan, Croatia.
Croatia has 1,246 islands, islets and reefs.
The Dalmatian dog breed is named after the Dalmatia region.
Croatia is home to the world's smallest town, Hum.
Stone from the island of Brač was used in the White House.
Croatia has the richest collection of Neanderthal remains in the world.
The torpedo was invented in Rijeka.
Zadar has a sea organ that plays music using ocean waves.

196.name: Cyprus
facts: Geographically in Asia but culturally and politically European.
The goddess Aphrodite is said to have been born here.
Commandaria is the world's oldest wine still in production.
Nicosia is the only divided capital in the world.
Cyprus is famous for Halloumi cheese.
There are more cats than humans on the island.
Cyprus enjoys around 340 days of sunshine per year.
It is the third largest island in the Mediterranean.
The name of copper (Cuprum) derives from Cyprus.
Cyprus is home to the mouflon, a rare species of wild sheep.

203.name: Czech Republic
facts: Czechs are the world's largest beer consumers per capita.
Prague is known as the City of a Hundred Spires.
Soft contact lenses were invented by a Czech chemist.
The word 'Robot' was coined by Czech writer Karel Čapek.
Prague Castle is the largest ancient castle complex in the world.
Sugar cubes were invented in the Czech Republic.
The country is famous for Bohemian crystal.
Charles University is one of the oldest in Europe (1348).
Škoda is one of the oldest car manufacturers.
The country is almost entirely surrounded by mountains.

208.name: Denmark
facts: The Danish flag is the oldest still in use in the world (since 1219).
LEGO was invented in Denmark.
No point in the country is more than 52 km from the sea.
Nine out of ten people in Denmark own a bicycle.
The concept of 'Hygge' (cosiness and comfort) was born here.
Denmark has Europe's oldest monarchy.
Hans Christian Andersen (The Little Mermaid) was Danish.
The Tivoli Gardens inspired Walt Disney.
Greenland is an autonomous territory of Denmark.
Bluetooth technology is named after King Harald Bluetooth.

233.name: Estonia
facts: Estonia was the first country to allow online voting.
Skype was developed by Estonian programmers.
50% of the country is covered by forests.
Nearly all public services are digitised (e-Estonia).
Estonia has over 2,000 islands.
Wife carrying is a popular sport that originated in the region.
Public transport in Tallinn is free for residents.
Estonia is one of the least religious countries in the world.
The literacy rate is 99.8%.
Kiiking (extreme swinging) is a sport invented here.

246.name: Finland
facts: There are more saunas than cars in Finland (3.3 million saunas).
Rovaniemi is the official home of Santa Claus.
Finland is ranked as the world's happiest country.
Finland has the highest per capita milk consumption in the world.
Finland hosts the World Air Guitar Championships.
It is called the land of 188,000 lakes.
Finland was the first European country to give women the vote (1906).
Angry Birds was created in Finland.
The Northern Lights can be seen in Lapland for 200 nights a year.
Finnish sauna culture is a UNESCO intangible cultural heritage.

250.name: France
facts: France is the most visited country in the world.
The Lumière brothers invented cinema here.
France produces over 1,600 types of cheese.
The Louvre is the largest art museum in the world.
French was the official language of England for 300 years.
The hot air balloon was invented in France.
It is legal to marry a deceased person in France (in special cases).
France hosts the Tour de France, the most famous cycling race.
Champagne can only be produced in the Champagne region.
The metric system was invented during the French Revolution.

276.name: Germany
facts: There are over 1,500 types of German sausages.
Germany was the first country to adopt daylight saving time.
The Christmas tree tradition originated in Germany.
Germany has over 20,000 castles.
Berlin has more bridges than Venice.
Germany is the largest economy in the European Union.
Large sections of the Autobahn have no speed limit.
X-rays were discovered by German physicist Röntgen.
Beer purity laws in Germany date back to 1516.
Movable-type printing was invented by Gutenberg in Germany.

300.name: Greece
facts: Greece has more archaeological museums than any other country.
Greece is the birthplace of democracy.
The Olympic Games originated in Greece.
Greece has thousands of islands, but only about 200 are inhabited.
Voting is legally mandatory for all adult citizens.
The Greek language has been spoken for over 3,000 years.
80% of the Greek territory is mountainous.
Feta cheese is a nationally protected product.
Greece enjoys sunshine for 250 days a year.
The word 'alphabet' comes from the first two Greek letters, Alpha and Beta.

348.name: Hungary
facts: Hungary has won more Olympic gold medals per capita than any country that has never hosted the Games.
The Rubik's Cube was invented by Hungarian Ernő Rubik.
Budapest has the most thermal springs of any capital city in the world.
The Hungarian language is unique and non-Indo-European.
Lake Balaton is the largest lake in Central Europe.
Hungary has the second oldest metro in Europe (after London).
The ballpoint pen (biro) was invented by a Hungarian.
Vitamin C was discovered by Hungarian scientist Albert Szent-Györgyi.
The Budapest Parliament is the third largest parliament building in the world.
Paprika is the national spice of Hungary.

372.name: Ireland
facts: The harp is the national symbol — Ireland is the only country with a musical instrument as its symbol.
Halloween derives from the ancient Celtic festival of Samhain.
Saint Patrick was not Irish; he was Romano-British.
The Titanic was built in Belfast on the island of Ireland.
The Guinness brewery has a 9,000-year lease.
The Wild Atlantic Way is the longest coastal road in the world.
More people of Irish descent live abroad than in Ireland itself.
Ireland has won the Eurovision Song Contest 7 times.
There are no snakes in Ireland.
The Obama and Kennedy families have Irish roots.

380.name: Italy
facts: Italy has the highest number of UNESCO World Heritage Sites (59).
Italy has two independent states within its borders: Vatican City and San Marino.
Modern pizza was born in Naples.
The University of Bologna is the oldest in Europe (1088).
Every year 500,000 people visit the Pope.
There is a fountain that distributes free wine 24/7 in Abruzzo.
Italians invented the piano, the thermometer and electric batteries.
Rome has over 2,700 years of history.
Italy has three active volcanoes: Etna, Stromboli and Vesuvius.
Christopher Columbus was Italian.

428.name: Latvia
facts: Riga has the largest collection of Art Nouveau buildings in the world.
Latvia is one of the greenest countries, with 50% forests.
Jeans were invented by Latvian tailor Jacob Davis.
Amber is considered the national gemstone of Latvia.
Latvia has one of the fastest internet speeds in the world.
The Ventas Rumba waterfall is the widest in Europe.
Riga's central market is housed in old Zeppelin hangars.
Latvia claims to have had the first Christmas tree in history (1510).
Ice hockey is the national sport of Latvia.
Latvia has a collection of over 1.5 million folk songs.

440.name: Lithuania
facts: Lithuanian is the oldest Indo-European language still spoken.
Basketball is considered a second religion in Lithuania.
There is an official perfume called 'Scent of Lithuania'.
The geographical centre of Europe is located here according to some calculations.
The Hill of Crosses hosts over 100,000 crosses.
In the 14th century Lithuania was the largest country in Europe.
The stork is the national bird and greatly loved.
At Easter it is 'Grandma Easter' who brings eggs, not the Easter bunny.
Fried garlic bread is the most popular snack in Lithuania.
Lithuania has one of the fastest public internet connections in the world.

442.name: Luxembourg
facts: Luxembourg has the highest GDP per capita in the world.
It was the first country to make all public transport free.
Luxembourg is the only remaining Grand Duchy in the world.
The national motto is 'We want to remain what we are'.
Luxembourg has three official languages: French, German and Luxembourgish.
Nearly 50% of the population is foreign-born.
Luxembourg City is a UNESCO World Heritage Site for its fortifications.
Skype has its European headquarters in Luxembourg.
Prime Minister Xavier Bettel was the first openly gay EU leader to marry in office.
Restaurant Chiggeri in Luxembourg has the longest wine list in the world.

470.name: Malta
facts: Malta has been a filming location for Gladiator, Troy and Game of Thrones.
Malta welcomes 3 times more tourists than its own population each year.
Traffic in Malta drives on the left, a British heritage.
The island of Comino has only 3 permanent residents.
Maltese is the only Semitic language written in Latin characters.
Malta has 365 churches, one for every day of the year.
Valletta is one of the smallest capital cities in Europe.
Malta's megalithic temples are older than Stonehenge.
The British George Cross is depicted on the Maltese flag.
Malta enjoys over 300 days of sunshine per year.

528.name: Netherlands
facts: 26% of the Netherlands lies below sea level.
There are more bicycles (22 million) than people (17 million) in the Netherlands.
Dutch men are the tallest in the world on average.
Amsterdam is built on millions of wooden piles.
Carrots are orange in honour of the Dutch Royal House of Orange.
The Netherlands is the world's largest exporter of flowers, especially tulips.
Schiphol Airport is located 4 metres below sea level.
The Dutch love salty liquorice, known as drop.
The Netherlands is home to major companies like Philips and Shell.
The Dutch national anthem (Wilhelmus) is the oldest in the world.

616.name: Poland
facts: Poland has 17 Nobel Prize winners in its history.
Marie Curie (born Skłodowska) was Polish.
Poland adopted the first constitution in Europe (1791).
Malbork Castle is the largest in the world by surface area.
The origin of vodka is disputed between Poland and Russia.
Pope John Paul II was Polish.
Poland disappeared from maps for 123 years.
The Białowieża Forest is home to the last wild European bison.
Poland is one of the largest exporters of amber.
It is common in Poland to eat pizza with ketchup.

620.name: Portugal
facts: Portugal has the oldest national borders in Europe (defined in 1143).
Portugal produces 50% of the world's cork.
The Vasco da Gama Bridge is the longest in Europe.
Port wine takes its name from the city of Porto.
Portuguese explorers Magellan and Vasco da Gama mapped the world.
Portugal is home to the world's oldest bookshop (Bertrand, Lisbon).
Painted tiles called Azulejos are a national symbol of Portugal.
Fado music is a UNESCO intangible cultural heritage.
Japanese tempura derives from a Portuguese dish.
Nazaré has some of the world's biggest waves for surfing.

642.name: Romania
facts: The Palace of Parliament is the heaviest administrative building in the world.
Romania is the homeland of the Dracula legend (Vlad the Impaler).
Romania has one of the fastest 4G speeds in Europe.
Romania has the largest brown bear population in Europe, excluding Russia.
Timișoara was the first European city with electric street lighting.
Famous sculptor Constantin Brâncuși was Romanian.
The Danube Delta is a UNESCO biosphere reserve.
Romanian is a Romance language, similar to Italian.
The Merry Cemetery of Săpânța is famous for its colourful crosses.
Romania has a rich tradition of folk costumes and crafts.

703.name: Slovakia
facts: Slovakia has the highest number of castles per capita in the world.
Slovakia has over 6,000 caves.
Bratislava is the only capital that borders two countries: Austria and Hungary.
The geographical centre of Europe is located in Slovakia according to one measurement.
Vlkolínec is a UNESCO World Heritage village of folk architecture.
Slovakia produces the most cars per capita in the world.
The Tatra Mountains are the smallest alpine mountains in Europe.
The national dish is potato dumplings with sheep's cheese.
The flying car AeroMobil was developed in Slovakia.
Slovakia's flag is very similar to those of Slovenia and Russia.

705.name: Slovenia
facts: Slovenia is one of the greenest countries in the world, with 60% forests.
Beekeeping is a major tradition in Slovenia (World Bee Day was inspired here).
The Postojna Caves are home to the 'proteus', an amphibian resembling a dragon.
The first married couple to climb Everest was Slovenian.
There is one vineyard for every 70 inhabitants in Slovenia.
Mount Triglav is the national symbol depicted on the flag.
Slovenia has one of the largest bear populations in Europe.
Lake Bled with its island is a world-famous icon.
Melania Trump is originally from Slovenia.
Skiing is the national sport of Slovenia.

724.name: Spain
facts: Spain has more bars per inhabitant than any other EU country.
Spain was the first global empire on which 'the sun never set'.
Spain produces 43% of the world's olive oil.
The Sagrada Família has been under construction since 1882.
Spanish is the second most spoken native language in the world.
Spain is the only European country that produces bananas, in the Canary Islands.
Spain is famous for the Running of the Bulls in Pamplona.
Spain is home to the world's oldest restaurant, Botín, opened in 1725.
The mop was a Spanish invention.
Spain is a world leader in organ donation.

752.name: Sweden
facts: Sweden has not been at war for over 200 years (since 1814).
Sweden imports waste from other countries to power its incinerators.
The pop group ABBA comes from Sweden.
Sweden is home to IKEA, Volvo and H&M.
Spotify and Skype were both born in Sweden.
Moose accidents are a real hazard on Swedish roads.
Nobel Prizes are awarded in Sweden (except the Peace Prize).
Fika (coffee break) is a sacred social institution in Sweden.
Sweden has 267,570 islands, more than any other country.
Sweden recycles 99% of household waste.

040.name: Austria
facts: Vienna hosts the oldest amusement park in the world still in operation: the Prater.
62% of Austrian territory is covered by the Alps.
The sewing machine was invented by Austrian Josef Madersperger.
Vienna is home to the world's oldest zoo (Tiergarten Schönbrunn).
The founder of Porsche, Ferdinand Porsche, was Austrian.
Austria is not a NATO member and is constitutionally neutral.
The national flag is one of the oldest in the world (1191).
Red Bull has Austrian origins.
Postcards were invented in Austria.
Mozart was born in Salzburg, Austria.

056.name: Belgium
facts: Belgium produces 220,000 tonnes of chocolate per year.
Brussels Airport sells more chocolate than any other place in the world.
The saxophone was invented by Adolphe Sax in Belgium.
Belgium holds the world record for the longest period without a government (541 days).
It is home to Tomorrowland, one of the largest music festivals in the world.
French fries are said to have been invented here.
Belgium has the highest number of castles per square kilometre.
Billiard balls were invented in Belgium.
The Smurfs were created by Belgian artist Peyo.
Brussels is considered the capital of the European Union.

quiz.heroSubtitle: Pick one of the 4 sections and answer 10 questions.
quiz.bannerSubtitle: Choose one of 4 sections and answer 10 questions in each category.
quiz.imageCaption: Related visual clue for this question.
quiz.shareHint: You scored {score}/{total}. Let your friends know!
quiz.shareMessage: I scored {score}/{total} in the "{category}" category on EU Puzzle. Try it too!
quiz.sharePopupBlocked: Popup blocked by the browser. Allow popups and try again.

whatIsEu.title: What is the European Union?
whatIsEu.description: Origins, values, treaties and member states.

euInDailyLife.title: How is the EU relevant to your personal life?
euInDailyLife.description: Rights, mobility, money and practical benefits.

euAgenda.title: What's on the EU's agenda?
euAgenda.description: Climate, digital transition, security and growth.

whatIsEu.question: How many member states are currently in the EU?
whatIsEu.explanation: The European Union currently has 27 member states.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "27". The EU evolved from the 6-country European Coal and Steel Community (1951) and the EEC created by the Treaty of Rome (1957). It became the European Union with the Maastricht Treaty on 1 November 1993, expanded in major rounds in 2004, 2007, and 2013, and now has 27 members after the UK withdrawal in 2020.

whatIsEu.question: Which treaty formally created the European Union in 1993?
whatIsEu.explanation: The Maastricht Treaty established the EU in 1993.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Maastricht Treaty". Signed in 1992 and entering into force on 1 November 1993, the Maastricht Treaty formally created the European Union and introduced EU citizenship. It also laid foundations for Economic and Monetary Union that later led to the euro.

whatIsEu.question: Which city is considered the de facto capital of the EU?
whatIsEu.explanation: Most key EU institutions are based in Brussels.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Brussels". Brussels became the main working seat of key EU institutions when the Commission and Council administrations concentrated there from 1958 onward. It later hosted regular European Council summits, and since the Lisbon era it has functioned as the practical political center of EU decision-making.

whatIsEu.question: What is the main purpose of the EU single market?
options: Remove barriers to trade and movement
whatIsEu.explanation: The single market allows free movement of goods, services, capital and people.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Remove barriers to trade and movement". The single market was completed on 1 January 1993, building on the Treaty of Rome (1957) and the Single European Act (1986). Its purpose is free movement of goods, services, capital, and people across participating countries.

whatIsEu.question: Which of these is an official EU value?
whatIsEu.explanation: Human dignity is one of the core values listed in EU treaties.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Human dignity". Human dignity is explicitly protected in Article 2 of the Treaty on European Union and is central to modern EU constitutional values. The Charter of Fundamental Rights, proclaimed in 2000 and made legally binding in 2009, reinforced this legal foundation.

whatIsEu.question: What does the EU flag with 12 stars symbolize?
whatIsEu.explanation: The 12 stars symbolize unity, solidarity and harmony, not member count.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Unity and harmony". The 12-star design was first adopted by the Council of Europe in 1955 and later by the European Communities in 1986. The number does not represent member states; it symbolizes completeness, unity, and solidarity over time.

whatIsEu.question: When was the euro introduced as physical cash?
whatIsEu.explanation: Euro banknotes and coins entered circulation in 2002.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "2002". The euro became a non-cash currency for financial markets in 1999, and euro banknotes and coins entered circulation on 1 January 2002. That launch was one of the largest currency transitions in modern economic history.

whatIsEu.question: Which statement about EU membership is true?
options: All European countries are automatically members
Membership requires meeting criteria and unanimous approval
Membership is decided only by the UN
Any country can join in one year
whatIsEu.explanation: Candidate countries must meet accession criteria and be approved by all members.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Membership requires meeting criteria and unanimous approval". Since the Copenhagen criteria of 1993, candidate countries must satisfy political, economic, and legal standards. Accession then needs unanimous Council agreement, Parliament consent, and ratification by all member states.

whatIsEu.question: What type of organization is the EU?
whatIsEu.explanation: The EU combines supranational institutions with member-state cooperation.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "An intergovernmental and supranational union". The EU combines shared institutions with national governments, unlike a classic federation or a simple international organization. This hybrid model developed from the ECSC (1951), EEC (1957), and the EU framework established in 1993.

whatIsEu.question: Which country left the EU in 2020?
whatIsEu.explanation: The United Kingdom withdrew from the EU on 31 January 2020.
whatIsEu.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "United Kingdom". Following the 23 June 2016 referendum, the UK left the EU on 31 January 2020. A transition period lasted until 31 December 2020, after which EU law no longer applied in the UK in the same way as before.

howEuWorks.question: Which institution proposes most EU laws?
howEuWorks.explanation: The European Commission has the primary right of legislative initiative.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "European Commission". The Commission has the main right of legislative initiative in the EU framework, a role rooted in the original Communities and confirmed by current treaties. This design was intended from the 1950s to promote common European interests beyond national positions.

howEuWorks.question: Who directly elects the Members of the European Parliament?
howEuWorks.explanation: EU citizens elect Members of the European Parliament every five years.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "EU citizens". Members of the European Parliament have been elected by direct universal suffrage since 1979, with elections every five years. This reform strengthened democratic legitimacy compared with the earlier system of nationally appointed delegates.

howEuWorks.question: What does the Council of the European Union represent?
howEuWorks.explanation: National ministers meet in different Council formations by policy area.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "National ministers of member states". The Council of the EU is composed of national ministers depending on policy area and has existed since the early Community system from 1958. It is where national governments co-legislate and coordinate positions with the Parliament and Commission.

howEuWorks.question: What is the role of the European Council?
howEuWorks.explanation: The European Council defines priorities and strategic direction.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Set overall political direction". The European Council gathers heads of state or government and defines strategic priorities. It began as regular summits in 1974 and became a formal EU institution under the Lisbon Treaty, in force from 2009.

howEuWorks.question: Which body interprets EU law to ensure equal application?
options: Court of Justice of the EU
howEuWorks.explanation: The Court of Justice ensures EU law is interpreted and applied uniformly.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Court of Justice of the EU". The Court system dates back to 1952 and ensures uniform interpretation of EU law in all member states. Through landmark case law and preliminary rulings, it has shaped principles such as primacy and direct effect over decades.

howEuWorks.question: How are EU laws commonly adopted?
howEuWorks.explanation: The ordinary legislative procedure requires both Parliament and Council.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "By Parliament and Council together". The ordinary legislative procedure, expanded from earlier co-decision rules and consolidated by Lisbon (2009), makes these two institutions joint lawmakers. This balances citizen representation and member-state representation.

howEuWorks.question: Which institution manages euro area monetary policy?
howEuWorks.explanation: The ECB sets monetary policy for the euro area.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "European Central Bank". The ECB was established in 1998 in Frankfurt and has directed euro area monetary policy since the euro launch phase in 1999. Its core mandate is maintaining price stability across participating countries.

howEuWorks.question: What does the EU budget mainly finance?
howEuWorks.explanation: The budget supports multiple long-term policy areas and programs.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Agriculture, cohesion, research and programs". EU budgets historically devote major resources to the Common Agricultural Policy (started in 1962) and regional cohesion instruments developed from the 1970s. More recent frameworks also prioritize research, digitalization, and green transition.

howEuWorks.question: What does subsidiarity mean in EU decision-making?
options: Decisions should be taken as closely as possible to citizens
howEuWorks.explanation: Subsidiarity means acting at EU level only when more effective than national/local action.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Decisions should be taken as closely as possible to citizens". The subsidiarity principle was formalized in the Maastricht era (1993) and further clarified in treaty protocols. It means the EU should act only when objectives cannot be sufficiently achieved at national, regional, or local level.

howEuWorks.question: Who checks that EU money is spent properly?
howEuWorks.explanation: The European Court of Auditors audits EU finances.
howEuWorks.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "European Court of Auditors". Created in 1975 and operating from 1977, this institution audits EU finances and checks legality, regularity, and sound management of spending. It later became a full EU institution under the Maastricht Treaty framework.

euInDailyLife.question: What allows many Europeans to travel without border checks?
euInDailyLife.explanation: The Schengen Area removed many internal border checks.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Schengen Area". The Schengen Agreement was signed in 1985, implemented through the 1990 Convention, and began practical border-free operation in 1995. It was integrated into EU law by the Amsterdam Treaty, in force from 1999.

euInDailyLife.question: Which EU program supports student exchanges?
euInDailyLife.explanation: Erasmus+ funds education, training and youth mobility.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Erasmus+". Erasmus began in 1987 and became one of the EU's most recognized mobility initiatives. Erasmus+ consolidated and expanded programs in 2014, and the 2021-2027 cycle further increased participation and funding.

euInDailyLife.question: What consumer right exists for many online purchases in the EU?
euInDailyLife.explanation: EU law provides a 14-day cooling-off period for many distance purchases.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "14-day withdrawal right". This right is rooted in EU consumer law and standardized by the Consumer Rights Directive (2011/83/EU), broadly applied from 2014. It allows consumers to reconsider many distance and online contracts without giving a reason.

euInDailyLife.question: What happened to mobile roaming fees in the EU?
options: They were abolished for most plans
euInDailyLife.explanation: Roam-like-at-home rules reduced/removed roaming surcharges in the EU.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "They were abolished for most plans". The EU's "Roam Like at Home" rules took effect on 15 June 2017, ending most extra roaming charges inside the EU. The framework was later renewed and extended to keep cross-border mobile use predictable for citizens.

euInDailyLife.question: Which right helps workers move across EU countries?
options: Right to work and reside under EU rules
euInDailyLife.explanation: EU citizens have mobility rights, with conditions defined by national and EU rules.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Right to work and reside under EU rules". Free movement of workers has deep roots in the Treaty of Rome (1957) and was strengthened through EU citizenship introduced by Maastricht in 1993. It allows many citizens to live and work across member states with fewer legal barriers.

euInDailyLife.question: How does the euro help daily life for people in euro countries?
options: Removes currency exchange costs inside the euro area
euInDailyLife.explanation: A common currency simplifies payments and travel in euro area countries.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Removes currency exchange costs inside the euro area". The euro started in accounting form in 1999 and as cash in 2002, reducing conversion costs and exchange uncertainty for households, travelers, and businesses in euro countries.

euInDailyLife.question: What does the GDPR mainly protect?
euInDailyLife.explanation: GDPR establishes rules on how personal data is processed and protected.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Personal data and privacy". GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) was adopted in 2016 and became applicable on 25 May 2018. It modernized privacy protections, strengthened user rights, and set a widely copied global benchmark for data governance.

euInDailyLife.question: What support can workers receive from EU social coordination rules?
options: One single EU pension for everyone
Mandatory same salary in all countries
euInDailyLife.explanation: EU coordination helps preserve social security rights when moving between countries.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Portability/coordination of social security rights". EU coordination rules (notably Regulations 883/2004 and 987/2009) help people who move across borders avoid losing pension, healthcare, or family-benefit entitlements. The system coordinates national schemes rather than replacing them.

euInDailyLife.question: What does the European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) help with?
options: Access to necessary public healthcare during temporary stays
Permanent healthcare rights in any country without registration
euInDailyLife.explanation: EHIC supports access to medically necessary public healthcare during temporary stays.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Access to necessary public healthcare during temporary stays". The European Health Insurance Card (EHIC) was introduced in 2004, replacing older paper forms in many cases. It helps insured people obtain medically necessary treatment during temporary stays under local public-system conditions.

euInDailyLife.question: How can EU product rules benefit consumers?
options: By setting common safety and quality standards
By fixing one price for everything
euInDailyLife.explanation: Common standards improve safety, comparability and trust across the single market.
euInDailyLife.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "By setting common safety and quality standards". EU harmonization work accelerated with the single market project and the "New Approach" era from the mid-1980s. Common rules and conformity systems, including CE-related frameworks, aim to improve product safety and trust across borders.

euAgenda.question: What is the main goal of the European Green Deal?
euAgenda.explanation: The Green Deal aims for climate neutrality by 2050.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Make the EU climate-neutral by 2050". The European Green Deal was launched in December 2019, and the European Climate Law (2021) made the 2050 climate-neutrality objective legally binding. It also set an interim emissions-reduction target for 2030.

euAgenda.question: Which program funds research and innovation in the EU?
euAgenda.explanation: Horizon Europe is the EU flagship R&I funding program.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Horizon Europe". Horizon Europe is the EU's flagship research and innovation program for 2021-2027, succeeding Horizon 2020 (2014-2020). It funds science, technology, and mission-oriented projects across universities, companies, and public bodies.

euAgenda.question: What does the EU digital agenda focus on?
options: Digital skills, infrastructure, rights and competitiveness
euAgenda.explanation: The digital strategy spans connectivity, skills, security and fair digital markets.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Digital skills, infrastructure, rights and competitiveness". The EU digital agenda combines long-term targets under the Digital Decade policy with major regulatory packages such as the DSA and DMA adopted in 2022. It aims to align innovation, market fairness, and citizen protection.

euAgenda.question: What is one objective of the EU Chips Act?
euAgenda.explanation: The Chips Act supports semiconductor resilience and production in Europe.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Strengthen semiconductor capacity in Europe". The EU Chips Act entered into force in 2023 and was designed to reduce strategic dependency in critical technologies. A key objective is to boost Europe's semiconductor ecosystem and resilience by 2030.

euAgenda.question: Which area is central to EU energy policy today?
euAgenda.explanation: EU policy emphasizes secure supply diversification and the clean energy transition.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Diversification and clean transition". Recent EU energy strategy combines security of supply and decarbonization, especially after the 2022 energy shock linked to Russia's war against Ukraine. Initiatives like REPowerEU accelerated diversification and renewable deployment.

euAgenda.question: What is one aim of the EU’s industrial strategy?
euAgenda.explanation: The strategy seeks competitiveness, innovation and resilient value chains.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Improve competitiveness and strategic resilience". The modern EU industrial strategy, updated in 2020 and 2021, supports green and digital transformation while reducing vulnerabilities in key value chains. It seeks stronger capacity in strategic sectors inside Europe.

euAgenda.question: Why is cybersecurity high on the EU agenda?
options: Because cyber threats to citizens and infrastructure are increasing
euAgenda.explanation: The EU prioritizes cybersecurity due to growing risks to services and critical systems.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Because cyber threats to citizens and infrastructure are increasing". EU cybersecurity policy has expanded through measures such as the NIS framework (2016) and NIS2 (adopted 2022). The goal is stronger protection of critical infrastructure, businesses, and public services.

euAgenda.question: What does cohesion policy try to reduce?
euAgenda.explanation: Cohesion policy supports less-developed regions and reduces inequalities.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Regional disparities". EU cohesion policy has long aimed to reduce economic and social gaps between regions, with major instruments evolving since the 1970s and 1980s. In the 2021-2027 period, cohesion funds remain central to balanced territorial development.

euAgenda.question: What is a key EU priority for youth employment?
options: Promote skills and access to jobs
euAgenda.explanation: The EU supports employability through skills programs and labor-market measures.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Promote skills and access to jobs". Youth employment measures include the Youth Guarantee launched in 2013 and reinforced in 2020. EU funding and policy coordination support training, apprenticeships, and transition from education to work.

euAgenda.question: How does the EU approach global challenges?
options: Cooperation through partnerships and common policies
euAgenda.explanation: The EU uses multilateral cooperation, partnerships and common frameworks.
euAgenda.correctAnswerDetail: The correct answer is "Cooperation through partnerships and common policies". The EU addresses global challenges through coordinated trade, climate, development, and diplomatic tools. Recent initiatives, including Global Gateway (2021), illustrate a partnership-based external strategy.
